Describe what you see. How are “seasons” defined in these plots? Is the seasonal shape consistent across years, or does it change? What physical process drives the May–June peak?

May and June are the times of year with the highest peak flows. This is consistent across both plots that show the mean blue lines higher for monthly flow patterns for one plot, and the year lines peaking is the seasonal shape by year plot in May and June. Snowmelt is responsible for the peak seen during this time. 2013 is a bit of an outlier with a high peak occuring is September and lower peaks in May and June. This is not surprising though given the 2013 September flood was a major event the governor of Colorado declared a disaster emergency on September 12, 2013 accross 14 counties. Across years there are changes in how high the peak gets and the exact timing of the peak, but the overall shape is the same where snow begins melting in April, peaks sometime in May or June, and then goes back down by July.

title: Lab 6: Timeseries Data
text:
4. Stationarity and Autocorrelation
Two diagnostics that directly inform ARIMA model selection:
Lag plot — plots Flow against its own lagged values. Strong linear patterns = high autocorrelation (ARIMA has something to work with). A diffuse cloud = no memory. Use log1p(Flow) — on the raw scale, peak months compress all other observations into the corner and hide the structure.


Warning: `gg_lag()` was deprecated in feasts 0.4.2.
ℹ Please use `ggtime::gg_lag()` instead.
ℹ Graphics functions have been moved to the {ggtime} package. Please use
  `library(ggtime)` instead.









Stationarity tests — ARIMA requires a stationary series (constant mean and variance). Test formally before fitting:


Warning in adf.test(log_flow_vec): p-value smaller than printed p-value



    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  log_flow_vec
Dickey-Fuller = -7.3292, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary



    KPSS Test for Level Stationarity

data:  log_flow_vec
KPSS Level = 0.50753, Truncation lag parameter = 4, p-value = 0.03997


What do the tests tell you? If the series is non-stationary, ARIMA will need d ≥ 1 (differencing). Does the lag plot support the same conclusion?

These tests use and alpha level of 0.05 to reject the null. The adf test gives a p-value of 0.01 meaning this test rejects non-stationarity concluding the test is stationary. The kpss test has a p-value of 0.0400 so the tests rejects stationarity concluding not stationary. These tests disagreeing suffests the series is a trend-stationary calling for detrending rather than differencing.

Use model(STL(...)) to decompose the time series into trend, seasonality, and remainder. Choose a trend window size you feel is appropriate and justify your choice. Extract components with components() and visualize with autoplot().

Describe what you see:

What does the trend tell you about long-term changes in Poudre flow? Is there evidence of a decline consistent with climate change or upstream demand?
What drives the seasonal component? (Hint: snowmelt, irrigation withdrawals, reservoir releases)
Are there any spikes in the remainder that correspond to anomalous years you can identify?


The trend was higher for a period of 2014-2016 and then it decreased. From 2018 onward the trend is oscilating around 100 where some periods it goes low when season goes high and then trend recovers to around 200. The lowest trend values are occuring in 2020-2023 suggesting evidence of decline. Seasonal component is regular fluculations. These are driven by snowmelt where season peaks when flow peaks. The season goes down following its peak where water is then being used for irrigation, and municipalities in the warmer months. A spike in the remainder occurs in 2013. This is likely due to the flood mentioned earlier that effected the Front range in September of 2013

Modeltime Prediction

Data Preparation
Convert the historic tsibble and future tibble to plain tibbles with a Date column for modeltime. All predictor columns must be present in both the historic and future datasets — this is what enables the 10-year climate-driven forecast.
Streamflow is strictly non-negative but right-skewed — snowmelt peaks dwarf base-flow months. To prevent models from predicting negative flows, add a log-transformed response with log1p(Flow) (log(Flow + 1), safe when flow is near zero). Fit all models on log_flow; back-transform predictions with expm1() (= exp(x) - 1) to return to cfs units.


Train / Test Split
Hold out the last 24 months (2022–2023) as the test set. This mirrors the assess = "60 months" logic from the CO₂ and Lees Ferry examples — the test set is always the most recent data, never a random sample.


Using date_var: date




Model Definition
Choose at least three models — one ARIMA and one Prophet are required. Store model specifications in a list:


Model Fitting
Fit all models to the training data on the log scale. Exclude the raw Flow column so models only see log_flow as the response — each engine handles date and climate predictors internally.

Calibration and Accuracy

Describe what you see. Use these benchmarks to interpret the numbers:

MASE < 1 — the model beats a naïve seasonal forecast (last year’s same month)
MAPE — intuitive % error, but inflated in low-flow months when Flow ≈ 0
RMSE — penalizes large errors heavily; compare to mean test-period flow for context
Do boosted models (ARIMA-boost, Prophet-boost) outperform their base versions? If so, the nonlinear climate signal is worth capturing.


MASE was the smallest for MARS earth model (0.45). MAPE was highest for the XGBoost error models for both ARIMA and PROPHET (MAPE>17.7). The lowest MAPE value belongs to the MARS (Earth) model MAPE=13.3. This represents the intuitive % error suggesting the MARS model outpreforms the others here. Root Mean Squar Error RMSE was largest for the XBOOST engines again (>0.7), while the MARS model was the lowest again (RMSE= 0.53). The lowest model preformed the best because RMSE measures the magnitude of error (difference between predicted and actual). The best RSQ was also for MARS (0.85) suggesting this model explains the data best. Boosted methods are not clearly out preforming their base models. While Phrophet boost model has a better rsq (0.79 vs 0.78) it loses in terms of errors (RMSE 0.75 vs 0.69) and MAPE (17.95 vs 0.588).



Test Set Forecast
Forecast over the held-out test period and compare predictions to actuals. Back-transform with expm1() to return predictions to cfs. Two details matter: (1) expm1(x) is the exact inverse of log1p(x) and always returns values ≥ 0; (2) confidence interval lower bounds on the log scale can be slightly negative — floor them at 0 with pmax(.conf_lo, 0) before back-transforming so the ribbon never dips below zero cfs:









Do predictions track actuals during the 2022–2023 test window? Are confidence intervals capturing observed values? Note any discontinuities at the 2022 boundary between training and test.

The predictions do track actuals during this test window. The confidence intervals do capture the observed values. PHROPHET w/ regressors, PROPHET w/ XGBoost, and regression with ARIMA visually underpredicted for both 2022 and 2023. All models seemed to underpredict for 2023. MARS earth model was consistently the closest predictor line to observed values.

title: Lab 6: Timeseries Data
text:
Wrap-up
Examine your 10-year forecast and reflect on the following:

Model agreement: Do ARIMA and Prophet project similar trajectories? If they diverge, what does that tell you about their different assumptions (statistical stationarity vs. flexible trend)?


These models do not have the same trajectories. There are a few projected years, 2024, 2026, and 2032 where phrophet with regressors is predicting the highest peak streamflow out of all models wile ARIMA remains more conservative. The ARIMA models, specifically XGBoost, remains relatively consistent across all predicted years. ARIMA regression moves a little higher for flow prediction, but it is still smaller than the peaks of Prophet with regressors. Prophet XGBoost has an interesting pattern where it has small peaks following the peaks that the other models predicted, other than this difference this model is conservative in its predictions. ARIMA rely on assumptions of stationarity so it makes sense that these models produce smoother, more conservative forecasts. This compares to Prophet which can handle trend breaks, missing data, and multiple seasonalities. Allowing for more flexibility allows those larger peaks.


Forecast quality: Which model would you recommend to CWCB for water-supply planning? Justify with your accuracy metrics.


I would reccomend the MARS Earth model. This is because its accuracy metrics were the best. MASE was the smallest out of the models (0.45), the lowest MAPE (13.3), the lowest RMSE (0.53) and the rsq was the highest (0.85). This implies that this model fits the data best, while also not inflating error terms. The visual plot also showed the MARS model most closely aligning to observed data 2022-2023 period.


Negative predictions: If any model predicts negative flow, explain why this happens and what you would do to fix it in a production context (hint: log-transformation of Flow before modeling).


This model does not have any negative flow predicts. In this analysis, applying a log transformation to the response variable (log1p(flow)) before modeling and then back-transforming with expm1() helps avoid this issue by ensuring predictions remain positive.


Regressor value: The MACA climate projections assume a specific emissions scenario. How does uncertainty in those projections propagate into your streamflow forecast?


Uncertainty over the climate emission reality of the future introduces more uncertainty into these forecasting models. If emissions increase more than projected then climate effects like temperature, precipitation, and drought could be amplified in unaccounted for ways. This could result in the forecast under/over predicting. Many forecast models involving climate change include different emissions scenarios (RCP 8.5, RCP 6, RCP 2.6) so it may be wise to apply this methodology here too.


Improvement path: What information not in tmax, ppt, srad would most improve this forecast? (Consider: snowpack, upstream storage, irrigation demand.)


SWE could possibly help improve this forecast as the Poudre is a snowmelt driven system. Having data on storage release from resevoirs upstream could help explain patterns (or lack of) in flow. This could be especially important if NISP is ever completed, which would divert water from the Poudre, but also aledgedly suppplement flow in drier times. This could effect what the hydrograph of the Poudre looks like in areas affected by the reservoir.

At this stage you have a working end-to-end time series forecasting pipeline — the same structural approach used by CWCB, USBR, and state water agencies for operational planning. The processes you learned in tidymodels apply directly here, and you can extend this framework with cross-validation (time_series_cv()), feature engineering (lag variables, rolling means), and hyperparameter tuning.

section: Going Further (Optional): Add an Upstream Regressor
text:
Going Further (Optional): Add an Upstream Regressor
In lecture we showed that adding Glenwood Springs flow as an exogenous regressor improved Lees Ferry forecasts because upstream conditions carry physical information the model can’t infer from time structure alone. Try the same approach here.
Download data from a headwater gage on the Poudre (e.g., North Fork Cache la Poudre, USGS 06746095) and add it as a predictor:


Code
upstream <- readNWISdv(
  siteNumber  = "06746095",
  parameterCd = "00060",
  startDate   = "2013-01-01",
  endDate     = "2023-12-31"
) |>
  renameNWISColumns() |>
  # floor_date returns the 1st of each month as a Date — same type as pf_tbl$date
  mutate(date = floor_date(Date, "month")) |>
  group_by(date) |>
  summarise(upstream_flow = mean(Flow, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

= "2013-01-01", : NWIS servers are slated for decommission. Please begin to
